CHAPTER 1
FLIGHT RADIOTELEPHONY OPERATOR LICENCE
1.1

General eligibility requirements
(1)

Except for a holder of a pilot licence, a person required to use
radiotelephony apparatus aboard an aircraft must hold a flight
radiotelephony operator licence.

(2)

An applicant for a radiotelephony operator licence shall:
(a)
(b)
(c)
(d)

1.2

Be at least 17 years of age;
Demonstrate the ability to read, speak write and understand the
English language in accordance with the language proficiency
requirements;
Comply with the knowledge and skill requirements, for flight
radiotelephony operator; and
Demonstrate a level of knowledge appropriate to the privileges
granted to the holder of the licence.

Skill and knowledge requirements
(1)

An applicant for the licence shall pass a practical and knowledge test
covering the following areas:
(a)
(b)
(c)
(d)
(e)
(f)

(2)

International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) spelling alphabet;
Departure and position reporting;
Obtaining meteorological information;
Transmission and procedures of distress and urgency signals;
Communication technique procedures;
The necessity for brevity in radiotelephony communication and
priorities;
(g) Pre-flight briefing;
(h) Classification of directional finding bearings;
(i)
Radiotelephony facilities and frequencies available in the flight
information region;
(j) Elementary knowledge of the relationship between wavelength
and frequency;
(k) Radiotelephony procedures and phraseology; and
(l)
The ability to use the radio equipment of the type installed in the
aircraft and including the ability to carry out emergency
procedures.
The results of the knowledge test of the applicant for the licence shall
be valid for six months after the applicant passes the test
2


1.3

Authority to Issue Licence
Director General, Kenya Civil Aviation Authority is the authority responsible
for the issue of flight radiotelephony operator’s license.
Licenses are issued to persons who qualify in accordance with requirements
laid down in CAP 90 “The Flight Radiotelephony Operator’s license,” and as
stated in 1.2 above.

1.4

Licensing of Aircraft Radio Station
The authority for approving the design and installation of radio equipment
in an aircraft is the Director General of civil aviation. This approval must be
in writing, while the Commissioner General of the Communications
Commission of Kenya (CCK.) is the authority for licensing the radio station.

1.5

Application for RTF License.
The following are the requirements to be met by a RTF applicant
(i)
(ii)

1.6

Complete application forms obtainable from the licensing section of
the KCAA No medical examination is required.
Photos:- Complete application forms forwarded to licensing section
with two un-mounted photographs of the head (1¼” x ¾”).

Fees
Since the fees are liable to variations, reference for chargeable fees should
be made to the latest AIC.
NOTES:
(i)
License may be renewed by just paying appropriate fee.
(ii)
No renewal fee is charged where the holder also holds a current
pilot’s licence.
(iii)
Where a practical exam is required a reduced fee may be charged.

1.7

Privilege of Licence
The holder of the licence shall have the privilege to use the radiotelephone on
board an aircraft. He/she must sign his/her name on the licence in ink in the
space provided.

3

book: Skymax Aviation — Flight Radiotelephony Operator Notes
subject: RT
text:
1.8

Radiotelephony Licensing Examination
The examination is divided into two parts; a practical and a written paper of
30 minutes duration. Both parts must be passed within a period of six months
and the standard pass mark in each part is 90%.

1.9

Exemptions from Examinations
Exemption from RTF licensing examination may be granted to:

1.10

(a)

Applicants who provide evidence of possession or equivalent
qualification.

(b)

Holders of a Kenya ATC Licenses.

Conversion of Foreign Licence
To convert any foreign pilot’s licence which does not include RTF Operator's
Licence, the applicant must sit for and pass a RTF examination before such
licence can be converted to Kenya Licence.

1.11

English Proficiency Test
It is an ICAO requirement that every pilot be tested and issued an English
Proficiency Certificate. The test comprises of the following:

1.12

(a)

About 5 minute interview on why you are working or have interest in
aviation.

(b)

About 10 minute conversation about aviation in general.

(c)

About 10 minutes listening to a taped ATC conversation (various
nationality accents – American, Arabic, Spanish, etc.) and selecting
correct responses from choices (A, B, C, D) given.

Maximum period of validity
The maximum validity period for a R/T licence is 24 months.

1.13

Renewal Requirements
A holder of the licence may apply for renewal of the licence if he/she has
exercised its privileges in the six months preceding the date of application.
4

CHAPTER 2
AIR TRAFFIC SERVICES
2.1

Definitions
Aerodrome: A defined area on land or water (including any buildings,
installations and equipment) intended to be used either wholly or in
part for the arrival, departure and surface movements of aircraft.
Aerodrome Control Service: Air traffic control service for aerodrome
traffic.
Aerodrome Taxi Circuit: The specified path of aircraft on the manoeuvring
area during specific wind conditions.
Aerodrome Traffic: All traffic on the manoeuvring area of an aerodrome
and all aircraft flying in the vicinity of an aerodrome.
Aerodrome Traffic Circuit: The specified path to be flown by aircraft
operating in the vicinity of an aerodrome.
Air Traffic Advisory Service: A service provided within advisory airspace to
ensure separation, in so far as possible, between aircraft operating on
IFR, flight space.
Air Traffic Control Service: A service provided for the purpose of:
(1)
(2)

Preventing collusions: (i) between aircraft in the air; and
(ii) on the maneuvering area, between
aircraft and obstructions.
Expediting and maintaining orderly flow of air traffic.

Air Traffic Services: This is a generic term meaning variously flight
information service, alerting service, air traffic advisory service and air
traffic control service.
Aerodrome Traffic Zone: An airspace of defined dimensions established
around an aerodrome for the protection of aerodrome traffic.
Alerting Service: A service provided to notify appropriate organization
regarding aircraft in need of SAR and to assist such organization as
required.
Altitude: The vertical distance of a level, a point or an object considered as
a point, measured from mean sea level.
5


Blind Transmission: A transmission from one station to another station in
circumstances where two-way communication cannot be established,
but where it is believed that the called station is able to receive the
transmission.
Broadcast: A transmission of information relating to air navigation that is
not addressed to a specific station or stations.
Flight Information Service: A service provided for the purpose of giving
advice and information useful for the safe and efficient conduct of
flights.
Flight Level: A surface of constant atmospheric pressure which is related to
a specific datum, 1013.25 hPa, and is separated from other such
surfaces by specific pressure intervals.
Height: The vertical distance of a level, a point or an object considered as a
point, measured from a specified datum.
Level: A generic term relating to the vertical position of an aircraft in flight
and meaning variously height, altitude and flight level.
Manoeuvring Area: That part of an aerodrome to be used for the take-off,
landing and taxiing of aircraft excluding aprons.
Radar: A radio detection device which provides information on range,
azimuth and elevation of objects.
Secondary Surveillance Radar (SSR): A radar surveillance system which
uses transmitters/receivers (interrogators) and transponders.
Transition Altitude: An altitude at or below which the vertical position of an
aircraft if controlled by reference to altitudes.
Transition Layer: The airspace between the transition altitude and the
transition layer.
Transition Level: The lowest flight level available for use above the
transition altitude.

6

2.2

Units of Measurement
The International System of Units (SI Units) developed and maintained by the
General Conference of Weights and Measures (CGPM) shall be used as the
standard system of units of measurement for all aspects of international civil
aviation air and ground operations.
Some non-SI Units are considered for permanent use with the SI Units. These
shall be used either in lieu of, or in addition to, SI Units as primary units.
Such units are: feet, nautical miles, knots, feet per minute.
The table of units shown below is used by the Aeronautical stations in the
Nairobi FIR.

For measurement of:

Distance used in navigation, position reporting, etc. –
generally in excess of 2 nautical miles
Relatively short distances such as those relating to
aerodrome (e.g. runway lengths)
+ Altitudes, Elevation and Heights
Horizontal speed including wind speed
Vertical speed
Wind direction for landing and taking off
Wind direction except for landing and taking off
Visibility including runway visual range
Altimeter setting
Temperature
Weight
Time

Units used
*Nautical Miles and tenths
Metres
Feet/Metres
Knots/Km per hour
Feet per minute
Degrees Magnetic
Degrees True
Kilometres or Metres
Hectopascal
Degrees Celsius (Centigrade)
Metric Tonnes or Kilograms
Hours and minutes, the day of 24
hours beginning at midnight UTC

Notes:
1. * One international nautical mile = 1,852 Metres
2. + Cruising levels are given in flight level numbers and not in altitudes

2.3

Time System
Coordinated Universal Time (UTC) is used by the air navigation services. This
is 3 hours behind the East African Local Time

2.4

Units of ATC
The units which provide Air Traffic Control Services include:
(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv)

Area Control Service (ACC),
Radar Control Service (RADAR),
Approach Control Service (APPROACH), and
Aerodrome Control Service (TOWER)
7


2.5

Use of SSR for ATC Services
All aircraft in the Nairobi FIR are required to carry serviceable Secondary
Radar Transponder with either Mode A, Mode C or Mode S capable of replying
to interrogations from Secondary Radar installations on the ground and set in
accordance with instructions from the appropriate Radar Units.
Validity checks will be requested by Radar Controllers to verify the accuracy
of level information relayed. In cases where the information shows inaccuracy
of more than 300ft, the pilot will be advised to cease transmitting on Mode C.

2.6

Special SSR Codes
In the current Mode A and Mode C Transponders, there are a total possible
combination for 4096 codes.
SSR Transponder Codes may be used to relay or convey specific special
messages. Some of important codes to remember are:
Code

To Be Selected When:

2000
2077
7500
7600
7700

Flying under IFR until assigned specific code by ATS Unit.
Flying under VFR until assigned specific code by ATS Unit.
Aircraft subject to unlawful interference (Hijacked)
Aircraft experiencing two-way radio communication failure.
Aircraft flying in a state of emergency.

(See standard phraseology for SSR communications at APPENDIX 3)
2.7

Flight at Aerodromes

2.7.1

Aerodromes not having Air Traffic Control units
(1) An aircraft shall not fly within an aerodrome traffic zone of an
aerodrome which does not have an air traffic control unit, except for the
purpose of taking off, landing or observing the signals in the signal area
with a view to landing; and the aircraft flying in such a zone for the
purpose of observing the signals shall remain clear of cloud and at least
500 feet above the level of the aerodrome.
(2) The commander of an aircraft flying in such a zone or moving on such
an aerodrome shall:
(a)

Conform to the pattern of traffic formed by other aircraft, or keep
clear of the airspace in which the pattern is formed;
8

book: Skymax Aviation — Flight Radiotelephony Operator Notes
subject: RT
text:
2.7.2

(b)

Make all turns to the left unless ground signals indicate otherwise;
and

(c)

Take-off and land in the direction indicated by the ground signals
or, if no such are displayed, into the wind, unless good aviation
practice demands otherwise.

Aerodromes having Air Traffic Control units
The commander of an aircraft flying in the aerodrome traffic zone of an
aerodrome having an air traffic control unit or moving on the manoeuvring
area of such an aerodrome shall:

2.7.3

(a)

maintain a continuous watch on the appropriate radio frequency
notified for the control communication at the aerodrome; and

(b)

not taxi, take-off or land without permission of the air traffic control
unit.

In VMC:
All aerodrome traffic are under the control of Aerodrome Control Unit.
Nevertheless, the onus of avoiding collisions rests with the pilot.

2.7.4

In IMC
Control of aircraft on the runway in use and in the air rests with the
Approach Control Unit.

2.7.5

Combined Services
At some controlled aerodromes, the functions of the aerodrome and
approach control may be performed by a single unit employing one set of
radio communications channels.

9


2.8

Aerodrome Control Service

2.8.1

Pre-taxi information
Prior to taxiing for take-off, aircraft shall be advised of the following
information, in the order listed, except for elements which it is known to
have already received.
(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv)
(v)
(vi)

2.8.2

The runway to be used.
The current surface wind direction and speed
The QNH altimeter setting
The air temperatures to be used in the case of turbine engine
aircraft.
The current visibility in direction of take-off if less than 10 km
The correct time.

Prior to take-off
The aircraft shall be advised of:
(i)
(ii)

2.8.3

Any significant changes in the surface wind velocity, air temperature
and visibility.
Any significant changes in meteorological conditions in the take-off
and climb out area.

Prior to entering traffic circuit
Prior to entering the traffic circuit, an aircraft shall be provided with the
following elements of information in the order listed:
(i)
(ii)
(iii)

The runway to be used
The mean surface wind velocity and significant variations
The QNH altimeter setting

2.9

Basic altimeter setting procedures

2.9.1

Take-off and Climb
A QNH altimeter setting will be made available in the routine take-off
instructions. Vertical distance during the climb will be by reference to
altitudes until the transition altitude is reached.

10


2.9.2

En-route
Aircraft en-route will be flown at flight levels at all times during an IFR
flight. The levels flown must conform to the semi-circular rule.

2.9.3

Approach and Landing
A QNH altimeter setting and a transition level number will be made
available to aircraft in the routine approach and landing instructions. The
vertical displacement of aircraft on initial approach above the transition
level will be controlled by reference to flight levels.
On penetrating the transition level in the course of descending, the
vertical displacement of the aircraft will be controlled by reference to
altitudes.

2.10

Essential information on aerodrome
Essential information on aerodrome conditions shall be given to every
aircraft. At certain aerodromes this information together with
meteorological data may be provided in the form of automated broadcasts
through an automatic terminal information service (ATIS). The information
shall include information relating to the following:
(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv)
(v)
(vi)
(vii)

2.11

Construction or maintenance work on or adjacent to the
maneuvering area
Rough portions of any party of the maneuvering area
Presence of water on runways and taxing
Parked aircraft or other objects on or immediately adjacent to taxiways.
Presence of other temporary hazards, such as binds on the ground
or in the air.
Failure of irregular operation of paid or all of the aerodrome
lighting system.
Any other pertinent information.

Visual Circuit Reporting Procedure
Rules regarding operation of aircraft at aerodromes have been stated in
paragraph 2.7 above.
Paragraph 2.7.1 (2) (b) requires that commanders of aircraft make all
turns to the left when approaching to land or after take-off unless ground
signals indicate otherwise. This is the procedure for standard circuit.

11

text:
In order that maximum use may be made of aerodrome for the purpose
of landings and take-offs, it is essential that pilots accurately report their
positions in the circuit when so requested. The Positions in which the
various reports should be made are:
Downwind

X

Base leg

Final
Long Final

(i)
(ii)
(iii)

Downwind:
Base leg:
Final:

(iv)

Long Final:

when abeam the upwind end the runway
immediately on completion of turn on to base leg.
After the completion of the turn on to final approach
when not more than 4 NM from the approach end of
the runway.
Aircraft flying a final approach of a greater length
than 4NM are to report “Long Final” when beyond
that range and “Final” when a range of 4 NM is
reached. (Long Final starts at 8 NM)

2.12

Use of Runways

2.12.1

Normally, only one aircraft will be permitted on the runway in use at any
one time, however

2.12.2

An aircraft may be allowed to land on a runway before the preceding
aircraft has cleared it if:
(i)
(ii)
(iii)

The length of the runway permits;
It is during daylight hours; and
The following aircraft will be able to see the preceding one clearly
and continuously until it has turned off the runway.

The Aerodrome Control Unit will have warned the pilot of the aircraft
ahead by giving the instruction “Land after……………………… (aircraft
type)” instead of the usual “Cleared to land.”
2.12.3

A departure aircraft may be cleared for take-off, or, an arriving aircraft
may be cleared to land, when the preceding departing aircraft:
(i)
is airborne and has retracted its undercarriage; or
12


(ii)

(In the case of a fixed undercarriage aircraft), is airborne and has
cleared the upwind end of the runway in use.

2.12.4

The pilot has the final decision as to which runway he uses.

2.13

Entering a Control Zone or Control Area
RTF Requests to enter a control zone or control area should be made
when at least 10 minutes flying time from the boundary, giving the
following information:
(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv)
(v)

call sign or aircraft identification
Type of aircraft
Position, level and flight conditions
Estimated time of arrival
True Air Speed

If aerodrome of departure is less than 10 minutes flying time from the
boundary, pilots should request clearance before departure.
2.14

Special Application of IFR
When observed cloud base at an aerodrome within a control zone is more
than 4 oktas below 1,500 ft or visibility is less than 8 km, all aircraft must
obtain an ATC clearance or special VFR authorization before undertaking a
flight within the control zone.
The following details shall be passed to an ATCU by an aircraft in flight
when requesting a Special VFR clearance:
(a) Call sign of the aircraft
(b) Type of aircraft
(c) Intentions
(d) ETA at the point

13


2.15

Crossing Airways
Pilots of powered aircraft who wish to cross an airway in VMC by day may
do so without compliance with full IFR requirements, provided that
clearance is obtained from the appropriate ATS unit at least 10 minutes
flying time from the intended crossing point.

Requests for crossing should include:
(a) Aircraft identification
(b) Aircraft type
(c) True track
(d) Place and estimated time of crossing
(e) Desired crossing level
(f) Groundspeed
2.16

Pre-flight action

(1)

A pilot-in-command shall, before commencing a flight, shall be familiar
with all available information appropriate to the intended operation.

(2)

Pre-flight action by a pilot-in-command, for a flight away from the vicinity
of the place of departure, and for every flight under the instrument flight
rules, shall include-

2.17

(a)

a careful study of available current weather reports and forecasts
taking into consideration fuel requirements; and

(b)

an alternative course of action if the flight cannot be completed as
planned.

Flight plan
Except as authorized by the Authority a person shall not commence a
flight if he has not filed a flight plan.

2.18

Submission of a flight plan

(1)

Information relating to an intended flight or portion of a flight, to be
provided to air traffic services units, shall be in the form of a flight plan.

14

book: Skymax Aviation — Flight Radiotelephony Operator Notes
subject: RT
text:
(2)

A pilot-in-command shall, prior to operating one of the following flights,
must file a flight plan:
(a)

any flight, or portion thereof, to be provided with air traffic control
service;

(b)

any instrument flight rules flight within advisory airspace;

(c)

any flight within or into designated areas, or along designated
routes, when so required by the appropriate air traffic services
authority to facilitate the provision of flight information, alerting and
search and rescue services;

(d)

any flight within or into designated areas, or along designated
routes, when so required by the appropriate air traffic services
authority to facilitate co-ordination with appropriate military units or
with air traffic control units in adjacent states in order to avoid the
possible need for interception for the purpose of identification;

(e)

any flight across international borders; and

(f)

any flight departing from an aerodrome manned by the Authority.

(3)

A pilot-in-command shall submit a flight plan before departure to the
appropriate air traffic services reporting office or during flight (airborne
flight plan), transmit to the appropriate air traffic services unit or airground control radio station, unless arrangements have been made for
submission of a repetitive flight plan.

(4)

Unless otherwise prescribed by the Authority, a pilot-in-command shall
submit a flight plan to the appropriate air traffic services unit(a)

at least sixty minutes before departure and shall be valid for sixty
minutes for instrument flight rules flights or one hundred and twenty
minutes for visual flight rules flights; or

(b)

if submitted during flight, at a time which shall ensure its receipt by
the appropriate air traffic control unit at least ten minutes before the
aircraft is estimated to reach the:
(i)
(ii)

(5)

intended point of entry into a control area or advisory
airspace; or
point of crossing an airway or advisory route.

Where a Through Flight Plan, containing such particulars as may be
notified is submitted to and accepted by an air traffic services unit in
15


respect of a flight through a number of intermediate aerodromes, this
regulation shall be deemed to have been satisfied in respect of each
sector of the flight.
(6)

An air traffic control unit may exempt the pilot-in-command from the
requirements of this regulation in respect of an intended flight which is to
be made in a notified local flying area and in which the aircraft will return
to the aerodrome of departure without making an intermediate landing.

(7)

In order to comply with the instrument flight rules, before an aircraft
either takes off from a point within any controlled airspace, or enters any
controlled airspace, or in other circumstances prescribed for this purpose,
the pilot-in-command shall cause a flight plan to be communicated to the
appropriate air traffic control unit and shall obtain an air traffic control
clearance based on such flight plan.

(8)

The pilot-in-command after he has flown in controlled airspace shall,
unless he has requested the appropriate air traffic control unit to cancel
his flight plan, forthwith inform that unit when the aircraft lands within or
leaves that controlled airspace.

16

CHAPTER 3
AERONAUTICAL PUBLICATIONS
3.1

Integrated Aeronautical Information Package
The aeronautical information is provided in the form of an Integrated
Aeronautical Information Package. The package consists of:
(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv)
(v)
(vi)

Aeronautical Information Publication (A.I.P)
Amendment Service to the AIP (A.I.P. AMDT)
Supplement to the A.I.P (AIP SUP)
NOTAM and Pre-Flight Information Bulletin (P.I.B)
Aeronautical Information Circulars (A.I.C)
Checklist and Summaries.

3.2

Aeronautical Information Publication (A.I.P)

3.2.1

This is the basic aviation document intended primarily to satisfy international
requirements for exchange of permanent aeronautical information and long
duration temporary changes essential for air navigation.

3.2.2

Amendment service to the AIP (AIP AMDT)
Two types of AIP AMDT are produced.

3.2.2

(a)

Regular AIP Amendment (AIP AMDT) are issued twice in a year. The
publication dates are on the first days of January and June every year
and are identified by a light blue cover sheet.

(b)

AIRAC AIP Amendment (AIRAC AIP AMDT), issued in accordance with
the AIRAC system and identified by a pink cover sheet and acronym
AIRAC. It incorporates operationally significant permanent changes
into the AIP on the indicated AIRAC effective date.

Supplement to The A.I.P (AIP SUP)
These comprise of temporary changes of long duration (3 months and
longer) and information of short duration which consists of extensive and/ or
graphics supplementing the permanent information in the AIP.

17


3.3

NOTAM and Pre-Flight Information Bulletins (PIB)

3.3.1

NOTAM
NOTAM contain information concerning the establishment, condition or
change in aeronautical facility, service, procedure or hazard, the timely
knowledge of which is essential for personnel concerned with flight
operations.
NOTAM are originated and issued for the Nairobi FIR and are distributed in
three series identified by the letters A, B and C.

Series A

National and international distribution.

Series B

International distribution to adjacent F.I.R. only.

Series C

National distribution only.

Checklist of valid NOTAM are issued monthly via AFTN.
3.3.2

Pre-Flight Information Bulletins (PIB)
These are a recapitulation of current NOTAM and other information of
urgent character for the operator/flight crew. They are available at
aerodrome AIS Units.

3.4

Aeronautical Information Circulars (AIC)
These contain:
(a)
(b)
(c)

Information on long term forecast of any major change in legislation,
regulations, explanatory or advisory nature liable to effect flight safety,
Information or notification of an explanatory or advisory nature liable
to affect flight safety, and
Information or notification of an explanatory or advisory nature
concerning technical, legislative or purely administrative matters.

Checklist of AIC currently in force is issued once a year
3.5

AIRAC System
These are issued on predetermined dates known as AIRAC System.
They consist of operationally significant changes requiring amendment of
charts, route manuals, etc.

text:
CHAPTER 4
RADIO WAVES
4.1

Electromagnetic Radio Waves
If an alternating current is fed to an aerial some of the power will be
radiated outwards from the aerial in the form of ELECTROMAGNETIC
RADIO WAVES.

4.2

Radio Wave Terminology

Cycle

=

one complete series of values

Phase =

point reached by the wave front at any instant during the
formation of a 360° cycle

Wavelength =

distance traveled by the wave energy in the direction of
propagation during one cycle; denoted by the Greek
letter, λ.

Amplitude

The maximum displacement from the mean value

=

Frequency =

Number of cycles which pass a fixed point in one second.

Hertz (Hz.) :

One Hertz is one cycle per second.

20


4.3

Speed of Propagation
Radio waves travel approximately at the speed of light:162 000 Nautical Miles per Second
186 000 Statute Miles per Second

4.4

300 000 000 Metres per Second
300 000 Kilometres per Second

Frequencies
Since frequencies in the radio spectrum are measured in many thousands
of Hz. The following prefixes are used:
1 KHz. (Kilo Hertz)
1 MHz. (Mega Hertz)
1 GHz. (Giga Hertz)

4.5

=
=
=

1,000 Hz.
1,000 KHz or 1,000,000 Hz.
1,000 MHz or 1,000,000,000 Hz.

Relationship between Wavelength and Frequency
The mathematical relationship between frequency and wavelength is:
λ = c/f

4.6

where:

c = velocity of propagation (300 X 106 m/s)
f = frequency (Hz)

4.7

Frequency Wave Transmission
(a)

VLF, LF and MF

-

(b)

HF

-

Are low frequency waves
They travel over the ground (ground waves)
and tend to lose energy with time.
Some waves are transmitted upwards.
They travel upwards and are reflected back to
the ground by the ionosphere (sky waves).
This gives them a greater range than ground
waves

(c) VHF, UHF, SHF and EHF -

These are very high frequency waves.
They travel on the line of sight.

4.8

Propagation of Radio Waves

4.8.1

For convenience radio wave propagation is classified as:
(a) Ground waves,
(b) Sky waves, and
(c) Direct waves

4.8.2

Ground Waves
Transmitted energy leaving the transmitter travels close to the ground
following the curvature of the earth using a property known as diffraction.
The amount of diffraction is depended on the wavelength and decreases
steadily as wavelength is decreased. Ground waves make it possible to
receive signals well beyond the visible horizon. Commonly used frequencies
are VLF, LF and MF. HF is also used to some extent.

4.8.3 Sky Waves
In sky waves propagation the transmitted waves are returned to earth by
refraction in the ionosphere. Ionosphere is that part of the outer
atmosphere where solar (and cosmic) radiation removes electrons from the
gas molecules and atoms resulting in positive ions and negative free
electrons. Since the sun’s energy is the main source of the ionization,
densities, thicknesses and heights of the ionosphere differ between day
and night. HF is the frequency commonly used in sky waves propagation.

22

book: Skymax Aviation — Flight Radiotelephony Operator Notes
subject: RT
text:
4.8.4 Direct Waves
Direct waves follow a path similar to the line of sight, and so their surface
range is limited by the curvature of the earth. Direct waves are present at
all propagation frequencies, but the term is more used with VHF and above
because at these frequencies the ground wave is very short and there is no
sky wave refraction from the ionosphere.
Maximum theoretical range (MTR) for VHF and UHF direct communications
can be calculated by:
MTR = 1.25 (√ Height of Transmitter + √ Height of Receiver Aerial)

CHAPTER 5
AERONAUTICAL MOBILE SERVICE
5.1

Carriage of Radio Equipment

5.1.1

A person shall not operate an aircraft unless it is equipped with radio
equipment(a) that complies with the law of the State of registry;
(b) required for the kind of operation being conducted; and
(c) capable of receiving meteorological information at any time during
the flight.

5.1.2

All aircraft operated under visual flight rules or instrument flight rules shall
be equipped with radio communication equipment capable of conducting
two-way communication with those aeronautical stations and on the
frequencies prescribed by the Authority.

5.2

Definitions

5.2.1

Aeronautical Mobile Service (AMS): This is a mobile service between
aeronautical stations and aircraft stations, or between aircraft stations.
NB: Emergency position indicating radio beacon stations or survival craft
stations may also participate in this service on designated distress
and emergency frequencies.

5.2.2

Aeronautical Station: is a land station in the aeronautical mobile service.
In certain instances, it may be located on board a ship or on a platform at
sea.

5.2.3

Aeronautical Fixed Service: A telecommunication service between
specified fixed points provided primarily for the safety of air navigation and
or the regular efficient and economical operation of air services.

5.2.4

Aeronautical Fixed Station: A station in the aeronautical fixed service.

5.2.5

Air-ground communication: Two way communication between aircraft
and stations or locations on the surface of the earth.

5.2.6

Air to ground communication: One way communication from aircraft to
stations or locations on the surface f the earth.

24


5.3

Discipline in the AMS
Highest standards of discipline shall be observed at all times in all
communications in the AMS as follow:

5.4

(a)

Standard radiotelephony phraseology shall be used in all situations for
which it is specified

(b)

Transmission of messages other than the authorized categories of
message shall be avoided.

(c)

Where it is necessary for an aircraft station to send signals for testing
or adjustment of radio apparatus the concord of the aeronautical
station must be obtained and such transmission shall be kept to a
minimum.

(d)

When it is necessary for a station to make test signals, such signals
shall not continue for more than 10 seconds and shall be composed
of spoken numerical (ONE, TWO, THREE, ETC). In radiotelephony,
followed by the radio call sign of the station transmitting.

(e)

Except as otherwise provided, the responsibility of establishing
communication shall rest with the station having traffic to transmit.

(f)

After a call has been made to an aeronautical station, a period of 10
seconds should elapse before a second call is made.

(g)

When an aeronautical station is called simultaneously by several
aircraft stations, the aeronautical station shall decide the order in
which aircraft shall communicate.

(h)

For any communication between aircraft stations on an ATS
frequency, prior permission of the aeronautical station shall be
obtained. Such request is not required for brief exchanges.

Categories of Messages
The categories of messages handled by the aeronautical mobile service and
the order of priority shall be in accordance with the following:

(a)

An aircraft shall not change type of RTF call sign during flight, except

6.15

Air/Ground Communications Failure
(a) When an aircraft station fails to establish contact with the aeronautical
station on designated frequency, it shall attempt to establish contact on
another frequency appropriate to the route. If the attempt fails, the
aircraft shall attempt to establish communication with other aircraft or
aeronautical stations on frequencies appropriate to the route.
(b) If attempts in 8.1.1 above fail, the aircraft shall transmit its message
twice on the designated frequencies, preceded by the phrase
“TRANSMITTING BLIND” including the addresses for which the message is
intended.

NB:
Note the difference between transmitting blind and
broadcasting: In blind transmission two-way communication cannot be
established but it is believed that the called station is able to receive the
transmission, whereas in a broadcast, the transmitted information is not
addressed to a specific station or stations.
(c) When an aircraft is unable to establish communication due to air-bone
equipment failure it should, when so equipped, selected the appropriate
SSR code to indicate radio failure. (A/C 7600).
6.16

Basic Procedures to be adopted in the event of two-way Radio
Communication Failure
(a) Continue the flight in accordance with the current flight plan to the
holding point at the aerodrome of first intended landing.
(b) Arrange the flight to arrive over the holding point at, or as close as
possible to the estimated time of arrival in accordance with current flight
plan.
(c) Land within 30 minutes of the time descent should have been started.
If unable to land within this time, but able to complete an approach and
landing visually, do so. Otherwise leave the vicinity of the aerodrome and
associated controlled airspace on notified routes and altitudes (where
applicable). If no altitude or route is specified, fly at last assigned altitude
or minimum sector altitude, whichever is the higher, and avoid areas of
dense traffic, then either:
(i)

fly to an area where flight can be continued in VMC and land at
a suitable aerodrome there, or

(ii) select a suitable area in which to descend through cloud, fly
visually to suitable aerodrome and land as soon as practicable.
7


(d) In the event of a missed approach:
(i)

land at the aerodrome of destination if this can be achieved
within 30 minutes of the time descent from the holding point
should have started or (if this is impossible)

(ii) leave controlled airspace at once in accordance with notified
procedures.
Note:
The message “Delay not determined” will not be considered to
be an EAT. Pilots whose radio fail after they have received this message
should not attempt to land at their planned destination aerodrome but
should fly to another aerodrome following laid down procedures, if any.

8

Special Air Reports
Special Air Reports shall be made by all aircraft on all routes whenever:

8.10

(a)

Severe icing or turbulence is encountered; or

(b)

Other meteorological conditions are encountered which in the
opinion of the pilot in command are likely to affect safety of other
aircraft. Such air reports should be prefixed “AIREP SPECIAL”

Additional Air Reports
Additional air reports shall be made if a specific request is made before or
during flight by a meteorological service for the flight. Such air reports
should be prefixed “AIREP ADD”.

CHAPTER 9
DISTRESS AND URGENCY TELEPHONY
COMMUNICATIONS PROCEDURES
9.1.

Definition
Distress and urgency traffic shall comprise all radiotelephony messages
relative to the distress and urgency conditions respectively. The significance
of these messages is to alert other stations the existence of the relevant
emergency situation for it to be offered the due priority. Distress and
urgency conditions are defined as:

9.1.1

Distress:

This is a condition of being threatened by serious and/or
imminent danger and requiring immediate assistance

9.1.2

Urgency:

A condition concerning the safety of an aircraft or other vehicle,
or some person on board or within sight, but which does not
require immediate assistance.
The radiotelephony distress signal MAYDAY x3 and the
radiotelephony urgency signal PAN X3. , shall be used at the
commencement of the first distress and urgency communication
respectively.
At the commencement of any subsequent communication in
distress and urgency traffic, it shall be permissible to use the
radiotelephony distress and urgency signals.

9.2

Radiotelephony Distress Communication

9.2.1

The distress message to be sent by an aircraft in distress shall:
(a)

Be on the air-ground frequency in use at the time;

(b)

Consist of as many as possible of the following information elements
spoken distinctly and, if possible, in the following order:
(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv)
(v)

Name of the station addressed (time and circumstances
permitting);
The identification of the aircraft;
The nature of the distress condition;
Intention of the person in command; and
Present position.
15


Note: The station addressed will normally be that communicating
with the aircraft or whose area of responsibility the aircraft is
operating.
(c)

9.2.2

Telephone calls concerning distress incidents may be made from any
telephone. They should be passed in the form EMERGENCY to
…………..

Distress Frequency
(a)

An aircraft making an emergency signal should address its first call to
the ATS Unit with which it has already established communication or
to one which is known to be within range.

(b)

Should an aircraft be unable to establish communication on the A/G
Frequencies already listed, every effort should be made to make
contact with an aircraft, land or ship station on either:
(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv)
(v)

NB: 1
2.

9.2.3

121.5 MHz (Emergency Locator Beacon –Aircraft: ELBA. This
assists in locating an aircraft crash site.); or
243 MHz (ELBA fro Military); or
500 KHz (International distress frequency); or
2182 KHz (Maritime Mobile Distress Frequency); or
Any other available frequency.
There is more chance of signals being heard on 500 KHz during
the international silence period at 15 to 18 minutes and 45 to 48
minutes past every hour.
The emergency frequency 121.5 MHz. may be used to practice
emergency procedures including simulated urgency but shall NOT
distress situations

Amateur Radio Stations
In the event of an aircraft having made a forced landing, in a remote region,
further attempts may be made to communicate with amateur stations on
either:
(a)
(b)
(c)

3650 KHz (night); or
7075 KHz (day); or
14150 KHz (day and night)

(b)

Imposition of Silence
The station in distress or the station in control of distress traffic shall be
permitted to impose silence, either on all stations of the mobile service in
the area or any station which interferes with distress traffic. The instructions
shall be addressed as follow:
“- All stations” ( or to one specific station)
- STOP TRANSMMITTING
- MAYDAY.”

Action by other Aircraft
(a)

Distress communications have absolute priority over all other

(ii)

It has itself to render assistance.

Any station which has knowledge of distress traffic, and which cannot
itself assist the station in distress, should nevertheless continue
listening to such traffic until it is evident that assistance is being
provided.

9.2.7

Termination of Distress Communications and Silence
(a)

When an aircraft is no longer in distress, it shall transmit a message
canceling the condition.

(b)

When the station which has controlled the distress communication
traffic becomes aware that the distress condition is ended, it shall take
immediate action to ensure that the information is made available, to:
(i)
(ii)

The aircraft operating agency concerned, or its representative, in

The distress communication and silence conditions shall be terminated

ENDED” on the frequency or frequencies being used for the distress
traffic.

Radiotelephony Urgency Communications

For the purpose of announcing and identifying aircraft used for medical

CHAPTER 10
SEARCH AND RESCUE (SAR)
10.1

Emergency Phases
For the purpose of SAR, aircraft emergencies are classified in the
following three phases:
(a)

Uncertainty Phase (INCERFA): – a situation where in
uncertainty exists as to the safety of an aircraft and its occupants.

(b)

Alert Phase (ALERFA): - A situation wherein apprehension
exists as to the safety of an aircraft and its occupants.

(c)

Distress Phase (DETRESFA): - A situation wherein there is
reasonable certainty that an aircraft and its occupants are
threatened by grave danger and require immediate assistance.

10.2

Circumstances requiring declaration of SAR Phases

10.2.1

Uncertainty Phase (INCERFA)

10.2.2

(a)

No communication has been received from an aircraft within a
period of thirty minutes after time a communication should have
been received, or the time unsuccessful attempt to establish
communication with such aircraft was first made, whichever is the
earlier; or

(b)

An aircraft fails to arrive within thirty minutes of estimated time of
arrival last notified to or estimated by air traffic services units,
whichever is the later, except when no doubt exists as to the
safety of aircraft and its occupants

Alert Phase (ALERFA)
(a)

Following the uncertainty phase, subsequent attempts to establish
communication with the aircraft or inquiries or other relevant
sources have failed to reveal any news of the aircraft; or when

(b)

An aircraft has been cleared to land and fails to land within five
minutes of the estimated time of landing and communication has
not been re-established with the aircraft; or when

(c)

Information has been received which indicates that the operating
efficiency of the aircraft has been impaired, but not to the extent
20


that a forced landing is likely, except when evidence exist that
would allay apprehension as to the safety of the aircraft and its
occupants; or when
(d)
10.2.3

10.3

An aircraft is known or believed to be the subject of unlawful
interference.

Distress Phase (DETRESFA)
(a)

Following the alert phase, further unsuccessful attempts to
establish communication with the aircraft and more widespread
unsuccessful inquiries point to the probability that the aircraft is in
distress; or when

(b)

The fuel on board is considered to be exhausted, or to be
insufficient to enable the aircraft to each safety; or when

(c)

Information is received which indicates that the operating
efficiency of the aircraft has been impaired to the extent a forced
landing is likely; or when

(d)

Information is received or it is reasonably certain that the aircraft
is about to make or has made a forced landing, except when there
is reasonable certainty that the aircraft and its occupants are not
threatened by grave and eminent danger and do not require
immediate assistance.

SAR Units
Whenever SAR operations are required, a Rescue Coordination Centre
(RCC) is established adjacent to the Nairobi Area Control Centre.
Type I Rescue Sub-Centres (RSCS) have been established in all ATC
Units; while Type II Sub-Centres are to be found at all Provincial Police
Headquarters

10.4

Notification of Rescue Coordination Centres
Notification shall contain as much of the following information as is
available in the order listed:
(a)
(b)
(c)
(d)

INCERFA, ALERFA or DETRESFA as appropriate to the phase of
the emergency;
Agency and person calling;
Nature of emergency;
Significant information from the flight plan;
21

Facilities Available
Facilities available for SAR may vary from time to time. An up-to-date file
is kept in the Area Control Centre.
The following are the facilities of permanent nature:
(a)

Kenya Aircraft: Kenya Air Force, Police Air Wing, Kenya
Airways, Charter Companies, Aero Club, Private
Companies/Owners, Par-Rescue Units

(b)

Ground Parties: Kenya Army, National Youth Service, Provision
Administration, Forest Rangers, Mountain Rescue, Kenya Police
and Game Department

(c)

Marine: Kenya Navy, Kenya Ports Authority, Coastal Shipping,
Police (Lake Victoria)

(d)

Communication (Radio): The SAR is responsible for SAR
operations within Nairobi FIR.

22


10.7

The International 406 MHz. Beacon Registration Database
(IBRD)

10.7.1

The system became effective on 16th January 2006. it is managed by the
COSPAS-SARSAT Secretariat as a supporting element of the satellite alert
system that detects and distributes signals transmitted by 406MHz.
Emergency Locator Transmitters. Signals transmitted by the system are
of digital data nature with a unique identification.
Note:

10.7.2

10.7.3

The COSPAS-SARSAT System will cease processing of 121.5/243
MHz. signals with effect from February 1, 2009. All 121.5/243
MHz. Beacons should be upgraded to 406 MHz. before the end
of this deadline.

Services provided by COSPAS-SARSAT System
(i)

Positions of activated ELT are relayed to SAR Units more quickly,
more reliably and with greater accuracy.

(ii)

SAR Authorities can interrogate a registration database and retrieve
the characteristics of a subject aircraft and contact details of the
ELT owner.

(iii)

SAR alerts can be resolved as either genuine or false by a
telephone call.

Registration
For the system to operate efficiently, owners should register their ELT
and the SAR providers should have access to the registration database.

CHAPTER 11
DIRECTION FINDING
11.1

Working of Direction Finding Stations
Direction-finding stations work either singly or in groups under the
direction of a main direction-finding station.

11.1.1

Direction-finding station working alone
Such a station can only determine the direction of an aircraft in relation to
itself. It should give the following, as required:

11.1.2

(a)
(b)

QTE
QUJ

(c)
(d)

QDR
QDM

True heading to be steered by aircraft, with no wind, to
head for the direction finding station.
Magnetic bearing of aircraft, form the station.
Magnetic heading to be steered by aircraft, with no wind,
to make for the station

Direction-finding stations working as a network (Group)
When direction-finding stations work as a network, to determine the
position of an aircraft, the bearing taken by each station is sent
immediately to the station controlling the DF network to enable the
position of the aircraft to be determined.
The station controlling the network gives aircraft’s position in one of the
following ways:
(a)
(b)
(c)

Position in relation to a point of reference or in latitude and
longitude using the signal QTE.
True bearing of the aircraft in relation to the direction-finding station
or other specified point, using the signal QTE and its distance for the
direction-finding station or point, using the signal QGE.
Magnetic heading to steer with no wind, to make for direction
finding station or other specified point using the signal QDM and the
distance from the direction-finding station or point, using the signal
QGE

CHAPTER 12
METEOROLOGICAL SERVICES
12.1

Responsible Authority
Meteorological services for civil aviation in Kenya are provided by the
Meteorological Department of the Ministry of Transport in accordance with
provisions contained in ICAO documents:
(i)
(ii)

12.2

Annex 3 – “Meteorological Services for International Air
Navigation”
Doc 7030 – “Regional Supplementary Procedures”

Area of Responsibility
Meteorological service is provided within the Nairobi FIR. Main analysis
and forecast centre is located at the Meteorological Headquarters,
Dagoretti Corner, Nairobi. It is responsible for the provision of synoptic
analysis, area forecast products, warnings and guidance material to
Aerodrome Meteorological Offices.

12.3

Observations and Reports
Meteorological observations and reports are made at:
(i)
JKIA ½ Hourly plus special observations
(ii) Moi ½ Hourly plus special observations
(iii) Eldoret
½ Hourly plus special observations
(iv) Wilson
½ Hourly plus special observations
(v) Malindi
Hourly plus special observations
(vi) Eastleigh
Hourly plus special observations
(vii) Kisumu
Hourly plus special observations
(viii) Lokichoggio Hourly plus special observations
(ix) Wajir Hourly plus special observations

12.4

Types of Services

12.4.1

Personal briefing and consultation for flight crew are provided only at
JKIA, Moi, Eldoret, Wilson, Kisumu, Lokichoggio, Malindi and Wajir. For all
other aerodromes consultation is available by telephone.

12.4.2

(a)

Limited flight documentation is normally provided for domestic
flights.
26


(b)

For international flights, documentation comprises a significant
weather chart, an upper wind and upper air temperature chart and
the latest available aerodrome forecast for the destination and
alternate aerodromes

12.4.3

For the planning of VFR flights departing from unmanned aerodromes,
pilots can obtain weather reports and forecasts from relevant aerodrome
units on notified telephone numbers.

12.4.4

Whenever possible, requests for weather forecasts should be made at
least 6 hours before the flight time and should include:

12.5

(a)

The route and/or aerodromes for which the forecasts are required.

(b)

The period of flight for which the forecasts are required.

Aircraft Weather Reporting Procedures
Captains in command of aircraft are required to make weather
observations and pass weather reports over routine channels to the
meteorological office.
Aircraft observations during flight are recorded on the AIREP form. Such
forms should be handed in at the destination aerodrome at debriefing.
Weather reports should be in accordance with provisions in Chapter 11 of
this document.

12.6

Transmission of Weather Elements
The elements of actual weather reports to aircraft will always be
transmitted in the following order:
(i) Station and time of report;
(ii) Surface wind, direction and speed
(iii) Horizontal visibility (or RVR reading when applicable)
(iv)(Present weather if significant)
(v) Cloud amount and base
(vi)Temperature and Dew point
(vii)
QNH (or QFE on request)
(viii)
Supplementary information in plain language
Notes:

(a) Unless special circumstances necessitates the following
words will note be included in transmission of weather
elements to aircraft: “Wind”, “Visibility”, “Weather
conditions” and “Cloud”.
27

Visibility more than 10 kilometres;

No precipitation reaching the ground, no
